notes: Decider run 2026-04-07T19:29Z. Patches this run: WIN-019 Cycle 2 (3 new issues), WIN-002 EXP-013 incomplete integration fix, WIN-005 critical re-issue (ISS-490, ISS-491, ISS-513 still unapplied).

patches.find: Numerical coincidence with no causal mechanism proposed
patches.replace: Claimed ratio (2.66) does not match any measurable geometric property of the real analemma
patches.rationale: ISS-527 (moderate, Cycle 2): Curmudgeon confirmed for two consecutive cycles that 'numerical coincidence' understates the problem. The stronger and more accurate claim is that 2.66 doesn't match any measurable analemma property at all (height ratio ≈2.25, width ratio ≈3.01, area ratio ≈6.48). 'Coincidence' implies the value is real but unexplained; it's not even real.

patches.find: The dome claims a 2.66 lobe area ratio but provides no derivation from dome geometry — the value appears to be a post-hoc measurement of the analemma's known asymmetric lobe structure. The asymmetry arises from Earth's orbital eccentricity (faster motion near perihelion produces the larger lobe). The actual lobe area ratio depends on the measurement methodology and integration boundaries; published analyses of the equation of time confirm the asymmetry is fully explained by Keplerian orbital mechanics. No causal link between this ratio and dome dynamics has been proposed, and the dome provides no mechanism by which its flat-disc geometry would produce this specific value.
patches.replace: The dome claims a 2.66 loop ratio but this value does not correspond to any measurable property of the real analemma: independent computation from published analemma geometry (Lynch 2012, Irish Math. Soc. Bulletin 70; Holbrow et al. 2013, arXiv:1302.7179) yields lobe height ratio ≈ 2.25, lobe width ratio ≈ 3.01, and lobe area ratio ≈ 6.48 — none equal 2.66. The prediction fails at the most basic level: the claimed value is simply not present in the observable. Notably, 2.66 ≈ 8/3, and the dome's own source listing records data_source='Spirograph', indicating the value is a mechanical gear-ratio parameter from the dome's epicyclic mechanism — not a measurement of the sky. This makes the claim circular: assume a gear ratio, read off 8/3 ≈ 2.667, declare it confirmed. No derivation from dome geometric parameters (disc radius, firmament height, sun altitude) exists, and the dome's monitor.py contains no validation logic for this WIN — the value is hardcoded as a static string in inject_ai_layer.py with no comparison against fetched data.
patches.rationale: ISS-527 (moderate, Cycle 2): Upgrade weak 'no causal mechanism' argument to stronger 'doesn't match any measurable property' argument. Add specific computed ratios (2.25, 3.01, 6.48). Add 8/3 spirograph gear-ratio insight. Add Lynch 2012 / Holbrow 2013 citations replacing deprecated NOAA reference.

patches.find: Analemma loop shape has area ratio of 2.66 matching dome model.
patches.replace: Analemma loop shape has loop ratio of 2.66 matching dome model.
patches.rationale: ISS-528 (minor, Cycle 2): Fix terminology mismatch. Dome uses 'loop ratio' (not 'area ratio') as listed in its data source. Using the dome's own terminology before showing the value is wrong is more rigorous — we aren't even mischaracterizing the claim.

patches.find: The ratio is a numerical coincidence arising from well-understood orbital geometry.
patches.replace: The value 2.66 does not correspond to any measurable geometric property of the analemma (lobe height ratio ≈ 2.25, width ratio ≈ 3.01, area ratio ≈ 6.48) and appears to be a spirograph gear-ratio parameter (8/3 ≈ 2.667) from the dome's epicyclic mechanism, not an observation of the sky.
patches.rationale: ISS-529 (minor, Cycle 2): detail_verdict_text should match the upgraded argument. 'Coincidence' implies the value is real but unexplained; the value doesn't match any observable property. Update to reflect the stronger framing.

for the full derivation.

The dome's own defense: The model's evolution page claims WIN-002 and WIN-029 are "complementary, not contradictory" — that the aetheric medium damps the Schumann resonance, and the damped measured frequency still back-derives the correct dome height. This defense is self-defeating: if the medium alters the measured frequency away from the geometric resonant frequency, then the altered (measured) frequency cannot faithfully encode the geometric cavity height. The dome model would need to independently measure the damping ratio and subtract it before using the frequency to derive height — but no such independent damping measurement is provided. This argument will be addressed in full in a forthcoming section expansion.

patches.replace:
for the full derivation.

The dome's 'complementary' defense fails on its own terms. The model's evolution page explicitly addresses the WIN-002/WIN-029 tension: "Not contradictory. Complementary. The dome medium damps the resonance (WIN-002), and the damped measured frequency still back-derives the correct dome height (WIN-029)." This defense contains a fundamental error in resonator physics. In any lossy cavity — electromagnetic, acoustic, or mechanical — the measured resonant frequency depends on both the geometric dimensions and the loss properties of the medium. When you invert f = c/(4H) using the damped (measured) frequency, you recover an effective acoustic height that folds in the medium's loss tangent, not the bare geometric ceiling height. The dome cannot simultaneously claim: (a) the aetheric medium shifts the frequency from 10.59 to 7.83 Hz, and (b) the shifted frequency faithfully encodes the geometric ceiling height via a lossless formula. These are mutually exclusive. If the medium matters enough to change the frequency by 26%, it matters enough to change the height inversion by 26%.

The numbers expose the contradiction quantitatively. If the dome's geometric height is H(0) = 8,537 km, the quarter-wave formula gives f = c/(4 × 8,537) = 8.78 Hz — not 10.59 Hz. The dome needs aetheric damping to bridge from 10.59 to 7.83 Hz (a 26% shift), but the formula applied to the dome's own ceiling gives 8.78 Hz, requiring only an 11% shift to reach 7.83 Hz. These are different damping ratios for the same medium in the same cavity. Meanwhile, inverting the measured 7.83 Hz gives 9,572 km — not 8,537 km. No consistent set of parameters links the dome's claimed height, the theoretical frequency, and the measured frequency. The 'complementary' framing papers over a three-way inconsistency.

Moreover, the dome provides no independent measurement of its aetheric damping coefficient. Without an independently determined loss tangent, the 26% gap is a free parameter, not a prediction. Any measured frequency could be 'explained' by choosing the right damping value after the fact. This is the same post-hoc pattern seen across the model (see Section 4.6.3).
patches.rationale: ISS-530 (major): EXP-013 was marked 'integrated: true' in the tracker but only the first ~2366 chars of the replacement were applied to wins.json. The tail — containing the complete 'complementary defense fails' rebuttal with lossy cavity physics and the three-way numerical inconsistency — was not applied. The current wins.json has a duplicated, placeholder version of this argument ('This argument will be addressed in full in a forthcoming section expansion' appears twice). This patch applies the full EXP-013 replacement tail and removes the duplication.

ISS-490 (critical): CHAOS-7 DOI is wrong. 10.1186/s40623-020-01163-9 is a different paper in the same journal. The correct DOI for Olsen et al. 2020 CHAOS-7 model is 10.1186/s40623-020-01252-9. RE-ISSUED from 2026-04-06T22-28.json — still unapplied.

ISS-491 (critical): WIN-005 monitoring tag should be 'hardcoded' not 'live_fetch'. Curmudgeon confirmed in both Cycle 1 and Cycle 2: monitor.py uses a generic SAA decay threshold at TTB station; inject_ai_layer.py contains only static metadata with hardcoded observed values. RE-ISSUED from 2026-04-06T22-28.json — still unapplied.

ISS-513 (critical): Wrong Terra-Nova DOI. j.epsl.2017.06.036 resolves to a different paper (Fukai & Yokoyama 2017 on Neodymium isotopes). The correct DOI for Terra-Nova et al. 2017 on geomagnetic field patterns is j.pepi.2017.03.002. RE-ISSUED from 2026-04-07T02-10.json — still unapplied.
